skill.name: database-migrations-migration-observability
skill.description: Build migration observability plans for CDC pipelines, metrics, dashboards, anomaly detection, and alerting.
skill.summary: Create monitoring and alerting guidance for database migration workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, SQLite files, and system reconnaissance are false positives caused by Markdown fences or benign migration examples. Real medium-risk behavior remains because the skill includes network posting examples, CI migration commands, secret references, and broad tool permissions, but no malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Configured Network Requests in Migration Tooling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill shows HTTP POST requests to Kafka Connect, Grafana, and Slack webhook endpoints. These are legitimate observability integrations, but they can send migration metadata and tokens to external services if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit and include configurable endpoints and an authorization token. The context is legitimate monitoring, so this is a medium risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: CI Migration Commands With Secret Inputs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The GitHub Actions example runs migration monitoring and production migration commands while referencing a secret Prometheus URL. This is expected for deployment workflows, but command execution and secret handling require user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and secret reference are visible in the workflow example. They are not malicious by themselves, but they can affect production systems if reused directly.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for a Community Skill
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The front matter allows Read, Write, Edit, Bash, and WebFetch. These permissions fit the skill scope, but they increase impact if generated migration code is applied without human approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools are directly declared in the skill metadata. The risk depends on runtime use, so the confidence is high for exposure but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at code block boundaries. These lines are Markdown fences for JavaScript, Python, and YAML examples, not executable Ruby backtick calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited location is a Markdown code fence or ordinary template literal context. No Ruby execution syntax is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported by Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines contain the skill description, a UTF-8 JSON deserializer, and a cross-plugin bullet. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic algorithm appears at these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text was reviewed and does not include cryptographic operations. The finding appears to be a substring or heuristic match.

low_findings.title: SQLite Database File Finding Not Supported by Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines reference selecting the current MongoDB database and a source database name in connector configuration. They do not reference SQLite database files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations use generic database variables in migration examples. No .sqlite, .db file path, or local database artifact is shown.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Not Supported by Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines contain a source host field and GitHub Actions migration identifiers. They do not run host discovery, OS enumeration, or environment reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are migration configuration and CI metadata. There is no evidence of reconnaissance commands or data collection beyond normal migration identifiers.

low_findings.title: Configuration Access Is Benign in Context
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited configuration access reads alert settings for an AlertingSystem object. This does not access environment variables or credentials directly at the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line reads a local config dictionary for alert settings. Secret handling concerns exist elsewhere in the workflow, but not at this line.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Requires Warning Only
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The static critical heuristic combines network calls, command examples, and configuration access. The reviewed file contains legitimate observability examples and no credential theft, obfuscation, or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying risky capabilities are present, but semantic review did not find malicious intent. Medium publication warning is more appropriate than a critical block.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP POST to Configured Service URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that post connector, dashboard, and alert payloads to configured service URLs. Users should verify endpoints and redact tokens before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The POST calls are explicit and tied to operational integrations. The risk is contextual because the endpoints come from user configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Migration Commands in CI
dangerous_patterns.description: The GitHub Actions example runs migration monitoring and migration commands in a production environment. Users should require approvals and dry runs before applying this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow commands are directly visible and include production migration execution. The example is legitimate but operationally sensitive.

content.user_title: Monitor Database Migrations in Real Time
content.value_statement: Database migrations can fail silently or create replication lag during cutover. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design CDC monitoring, metrics, dashboards, and alerts.
actual_capabilities: Designs observable MongoDB migration flows with logging and Prometheus metrics.
Outlines CDC pipelines using Debezium, Kafka consumers, and replication lag metrics.
Creates monitoring plans for throughput, error rates, data lag, and anomaly detection.
Drafts Grafana dashboard structures for migration progress and health signals.
Provides CI workflow patterns for starting monitors and checking migration health.
limitations: Does not provide a complete runnable migration platform by itself.
Example code requires project-specific imports, credentials, and error handling.
Network integrations must be reviewed before use with production data.
Dashboards and thresholds need tuning for each database and workload.

use_cases.title: Plan a monitored migration rollout
use_cases.description: Define metrics, alerts, and dashboards before moving database traffic between systems.

use_cases.title: Track CDC pipeline health
use_cases.description: Monitor event throughput, consumer lag, replication delay, and failed change events.

use_cases.title: Add deployment health gates
use_cases.description: Build CI checks that stop rollout when migration lag or error rates exceed limits.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic migration dashboard
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one database migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a simple dashboard for a PostgreSQL to MongoDB migration. Include progress, errors, and lag metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Design CDC monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a CDC monitoring plan for Debezium and Kafka. Include consumer lag, event counts, and alert thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Build migration alerting rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to define Prometheus alerts for a production migration. Cover throughput drops, error rates, lag, and stuck jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Review an enterprise migration observability design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my migration observability architecture. Identify missing metrics, weak alerts, unsafe automation, and cutover risks.

output_examples.input: Create observability for a zero downtime migration.
output_examples.output: A monitoring plan with migration duration, rows processed, replication lag, error rate, anomaly handling, and dashboard sections.

output_examples.input: Add alerts for a Debezium CDC pipeline.
output_examples.output: Alert recommendations for consumer lag, connector failures, delayed heartbeats, high error rates, and throughput drops.

output_examples.input: Prepare migration health checks for CI.
output_examples.output: A deployment checklist with preflight monitoring, migration execution gates, lag thresholds, and rollback signals.

best_practices: Review generated network endpoints, tokens, and webhook destinations before running any example code.
Use dry runs and non-production databases before connecting monitoring automation to production migrations.
Tune alert thresholds with baseline workload data instead of copying default values directly.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated migration commands against production without approval gates.
Do not send raw row data or secrets to alert channels or dashboards.
Do not treat example dashboards as complete compliance or audit evidence.

faq.question: What databases does this skill target?
faq.answer: The examples focus on MongoDB, PostgreSQL CDC, Kafka, Prometheus, and Grafana. The monitoring patterns can adapt to other databases.

faq.question: Can it create production-ready code?
faq.answer: It can draft useful examples and plans, but engineers must add validation, credentials handling, tests, and deployment safeguards.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The guidance includes network integrations for Kafka Connect, Grafana, and Slack. Review all destinations before using them.

faq.question: Can it help with zero downtime migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on lag tracking, health checks, CDC monitoring, and alerting that support safer cutover decisions.

faq.question: Does it replace database migration tools?
faq.answer: No. It complements migration tools by adding monitoring, dashboards, anomaly detection, and operational checks.

faq.question: What should teams verify first?
faq.answer: Verify credentials handling, endpoint ownership, alert routing, rollback criteria, and non-production test results.
